name: Dowsing
description: Dowsing, also known as divining, is a pseudoscientific practice that involves using a forked stick, rod, pendulum, or other handheld device to locate groundwater, buried metals, gemstones, oil, gravesites, and other objects or substances. Proponents believe that dowsing tools are sensitive to subtle energies or vibrations emanating from the target, allowing the dowser to detect its presence even when it is hidden from view. The origins of dowsing can be traced back to medieval Germany, where it was primarily used for mining. Over time, it spread to other parts of the world and became associated with various beliefs and practices. Dowsers often claim high success rates, attributing their abilities to innate talent, spiritual connection, or sensitivity to electromagnetic fields. However, mainstream science largely dismisses dowsing as a placebo effect, ideomotor movement, or skillful observation. Numerous controlled experiments have failed to demonstrate the effectiveness of dowsing beyond chance levels, and its underlying mechanisms remain unexplained by established scientific principles. Despite the lack of scientific support, dowsing continues to be practiced by many individuals and groups around the world, particularly in rural areas where access to modern technology may be limited.
subtitle: Locating hidden objects through intuitive means

claims.claim: Dowsing can reliably locate groundwater sources.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal evidence from dowsers who have successfully located water wells.
claims.criticism: Controlled experiments have not shown dowsing to be more effective than random chance at locating water. Geological surveys and scientific methods are far more reliable.

claims.claim: Dowsing tools react to subtle energies emitted by the target substance.
claims.evidence: Dowsers report feeling a tug or movement in the tool when near the target.
claims.criticism: The movement of the tool is likely due to the ideomotor effect, where subconscious muscle movements cause involuntary actions. No scientifically validated energy field has been identified that explains dowsing's supposed efficacy.

claims.claim: Dowsing can be used to diagnose illnesses and identify health problems.
claims.evidence: Some dowsers claim to be able to detect imbalances in the body's energy fields and identify potential health risks.
claims.criticism: Medical diagnosis should be based on scientific evidence and clinical evaluation. Dowsing has no proven medical value and should not be used as a substitute for professional medical care.

keyFigures.name: Martin Luther
keyFigures.role: Condemned dowsing as a violation of the First Commandment.

keyFigures.name: Christopher Bird
keyFigures.role: Advocate for dowsing and author of 'The Divining Hand'.

keyFigures.name: James Randi
keyFigures.role: Magician and scientific skeptic who offered a large reward for proof of dowsing ability, which was never claimed.

mythologyConnections: Belief in earth energies and spirits

timeline.event: Dowsing documented in Germany for mining purposes.
Dowsing used in England to locate mineral deposits.
Continued use of dowsing for water location, particularly in rural areas.
Dowsing remains a popular practice despite lack of scientific validation.

sources.author: Barrett, William
sources.work: On the Evidence for the So-Called Divining Rod
sources.title: Barrett, William
